segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an upbeat electronic music track playing over a dark-themed Logic Pro X interface, displaying two software instrument tracks, a Loop Browser, and transport controls set to 120 BPM. The music continues as the scene transitions to a stylized, dark, particle-filled animation featuring a gold 'DJ NEO' logo with the tagline 'LEARN ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' and a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button, before the music fades out with a synth flourish and a whoosh sound effect. A bold black screen then appears, presenting the title 'LOGIC PRO INTRO CLASS #CLASS-1' in red and white text. A male voice, identified as DJ Neo, then greets viewers in Hindi, welcoming them to his channel and explaining that after a long absence, he is fulfilling requests to create introductory videos on Logic Pro X, which he describes as a "बहुत ही बेहतरीन डिजिटल ऑडियो वर्कस्टेशन" (very excellent Digital Audio Workstation). As he speaks, the video returns to the Logic Pro X interface, where a 'New Track' dialog box is open, showing the 'Software Instrument' tab selected with 'Empty Channel Strip' chosen and the 'Create' button highlighted, setting the stage for the instructional content as DJ Neo continues to explain that this video will provide an overview of Logic Pro and how to work with it.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with a screen recording of Logic Pro X, showing the interface with two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2' in the track list, alongside a Loop Browser on the right displaying various string and drum loops. The user interface is dark-themed, with visible transport controls and a tempo set to 120 BPM. The scene transitions to a stylized, dark, particle-filled animation featuring the 'DJ NEO' logo with the tagline 'LEARN ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' and a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button at the bottom. This is followed by a bold black screen with red and white text reading 'LOGIC PRO INTRO CLASS #CLASS-1'. The video then returns to Logic Pro X, where a 'New Track' dialog box is open, showing options for 'Software Instrument', 'Audio', and 'Drummer'. The 'Software Instrument' tab is selected, with 'Empty Channel Strip' chosen as the instrument, and the 'Create' button is highlighted. The interface remains static, suggesting the user is about to create a new track. The overall atmosphere is instructional, with a focus on introducing Logic Pro X to beginners.

audio_updated_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface, explicitly identified by the speaker, DJ Neo, as 'Logic Pro X' and a 'बहुत ही बेहतरीन डिजिटल ऑडियो वर्कस्टेशन' (very excellent Digital Audio Workstation), showing two software instrument tracks, a Loop Browser, transport controls at 120 BPM, and later a 'New Track' dialog box with 'Software Instrument' selected.
audio_updated_entities.DJ NEO Logo Animation: A dark, particle-filled animation featuring a gold 'DJ NEO' logo with the tagline 'LEARN ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' and a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button, representing the channel of the speaker, DJ Neo, who welcomes viewers and introduces the video's topic.
audio_updated_entities.Intro Title Screen: A black screen with bold red and white text reading 'LOGIC PRO INTRO CLASS #CLASS-1', serving as a title card for the tutorial whose purpose is explained by DJ Neo in the accompanying audio.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the dark-themed Logic Pro X Interface, where a New Track Dialog Box is prominently displayed. A male voice, speaking in Hindi, introduces the software, stating, "abhi Logic Pro hai... main already Logic Pro khol ke rakha hoon," and then details the options available in the dialog box, mentioning "software instrument, audio, drummer, external MIDI, guitar aur bass," which are visible as tabs. The dialog box is set to 'Software Instrument' with 'Empty Channel Strip' selected. The speaker elaborates on the 'Instrument' dropdown, noting that one can choose an "empty channel strip ya aap ek default patch bhi le sakte ho," and explains his preference, "main hamesha empty channel strip leta hoon kyunki mujhe isme zyada comfort lagta hai." As he speaks about other instrument options like "keyboard ya kuch bhi aap ya synthesizer," the user visually scrolls through the 'Instrument' dropdown menu, revealing options such as 'Alchemy', 'Drum Synth', 'ES1', and 'Vintage Electric Piano'. After reviewing, the user re-selects 'Empty Channel Strip'. The speaker then announces his intention to create a track, saying, "to abhi chalte hain hum log ek track banate hain, mujhe abhi ek track banana hai," and instructs, "create software instrument track." The user clicks the 'Create' button, closing the dialog box. The speaker confirms the action, "to hamara aap dekh sakte ho ki idhar ek instrument track create ho chuka hai," as a new software instrument track labeled 'Inst 1' appears on the interface. The interface now shows the newly created track with its channel strip, including controls for EQ, MIDI FX, and volume, while the main timeline remains empty, ready for recording or editing, which the speaker refers to as "yeh Logic Pro ka arrangement hai."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with the Logic Pro X Interface displaying a dark-themed digital audio workstation. A New Track Dialog Box is prominently open, set to 'Software Instrument' with 'Empty Channel Strip' selected. The user interacts with the dialog box, scrolling through the 'Instrument' dropdown menu to explore options like 'Alchemy', 'Drum Synth', 'ES1', and 'Vintage Electric Piano'. After reviewing the list, the user selects 'Empty Channel Strip' again and clicks the 'Create' button, which closes the dialog box and adds a new software instrument track labeled 'Inst 1' to the project. The interface now shows the newly created track with its channel strip, including controls for EQ, MIDI FX, and volume, while the main timeline remains empty, ready for recording or editing.

active_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface showing a newly created software instrument track labeled 'Inst 1', with a channel strip containing EQ, MIDI FX, and volume controls, and an empty timeline ready for use.
active_entities.New Track Dialog Box: A pop-up window in Logic Pro X with tabs for 'Software Instrument', 'Audio', and 'Drummer', currently set to 'Software Instrument' with 'Empty Channel Strip' selected and the 'Create' button clicked to finalize track creation.

audio_updated_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface, confirmed by a male speaker in Hindi as 'Logic Pro' and referred to as its 'arrangement', showing a newly created software instrument track labeled 'Inst 1', with a channel strip containing EQ, MIDI FX, and volume controls, and an empty timeline ready for use.
audio_updated_entities.New Track Dialog Box: A pop-up window in Logic Pro X with tabs for 'Software Instrument', 'Audio', and 'Drummer', currently set to 'Software Instrument' with 'Empty Channel Strip' selected. A male speaker in Hindi details the options presented, including 'software instrument, audio, drummer, external MIDI, guitar aur bass', and explains the choice between 'empty channel strip' and 'default patch', stating his preference for the former before the 'Create' button is clicked to finalize track creation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens on a dark-themed Logic Pro X Interface, initially displaying a single software instrument track labeled 'Inst 1' with an empty timeline. A male speaker, speaking in Hindi, begins by explaining how tracks are created and layered in digital audio workstations like FL Studio, drawing parallels to the current interface. As the user adds a second track, 'Inst 2', creating two parallel tracks, the speaker highlights the 'inspector button' on the left side of the interface and explains that each track, when created, gets its own 'mixer window'. He demonstrates this by selecting 'Inst 1' and then 'Inst 2', showing how their respective 'channel strips' appear, ultimately feeding into a 'stereo output'. The user then toggles the record enable buttons on both tracks. Subsequently, the user navigates to the 'Logic Pro' menu and selects 'About Logic Pro'. The speaker, referring to the interface, corrects himself from 'Logic Pro X' to 'Logic Pro' and explicitly points out the displayed version as 10.6.1 in the pop-up window. After closing this window, the user clicks on the time display in the control bar. The speaker continues, referring to this area as the 'transport window', explaining that the display can be customized. The user then selects 'Beats & Time (Large)' from the dropdown, changing the time display to '01:00:00:00.00' in a larger font, aligning with the speaker's explanation of display customization.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with a view of the Logic Pro X Interface, showing a newly created software instrument track labeled 'Inst 1' with an empty timeline. The user interacts with the interface by adding a second track, 'Inst 2', resulting in two parallel software instrument tracks visible in the track list. The user then toggles the record enable button (red 'R') on both tracks, alternating between enabling and disabling recording for each. Subsequently, the user accesses the 'Logic Pro' menu from the top-left corner and selects 'About Logic Pro', revealing a pop-up window displaying version 10.6.1 along with copyright and technology credits. After closing the pop-up, the user clicks on the time display in the control bar, opening a dropdown menu with time display options such as 'Beats & Project', 'Beats & Time (Large)', 'Time', and 'Custom'. The user selects 'Beats & Time (Large)', changing the time display to show '01:00:00:00.00' in a larger font. The interface remains dark-themed throughout, with all actions focused on track management and display customization within the DAW.

active_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with both tracks having their record enable buttons toggled on and off. The timeline remains empty, and the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format after user selection.

audio_updated_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface, initially showing 'Inst 1' and then 'Inst 2', with both tracks having their record enable buttons toggled. The speaker, in Hindi, explains track creation and layering, points out the 'inspector button' on the left, and describes how selecting 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2' reveals their respective 'channel strips' in a 'mixer window' that feeds into a 'stereo output'. The 'About Logic Pro' pop-up confirms the software version as 10.6.1, and the speaker clarifies the software's name as 'Logic Pro'. The control bar, referred to by the speaker as the 'transport window', shows the time display changed to 'Beats & Time (Large)' after user selection, demonstrating customization options.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Logic Pro X Interface displayed in its default dark theme, showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with their record enable buttons being toggled on and off. A male voice-over begins, pointing out the time display, which the user then changes from 'Beats & Project (Large)' to 'Beats & Time (Large)' via the control bar. The speaker continues, identifying the metronome and countdown timer buttons, and then the loop function. As the user clicks the play button, a metronome click sound begins, and the playhead moves across the empty timeline, while the speaker explains how to turn off the metronome and loop, and mentions the 'Enter' key shortcut to return the playhead to the start, calling it his favorite function. After stopping playback, the user navigates to the mixer view by clicking the 'Mix' button, which the speaker explains can also be accessed via the 'X' shortcut in Logic Pro. The mixer reveals channel strips for 'Inst 1', 'Inst 2', 'Stereo Out', and 'Master', each with faders, pan controls, and automation settings. The speaker elaborates that 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2' are the two tracks created, and the 'Stereo Out' is for mixing and mastering plugins, leading to the 'Master' output. The user briefly hovers over the 'Inst 1' channel strip before returning to the Tracks view, prompted by the speaker's mention of pressing 'X' again. Throughout the segment, the interface remains static with no audio or MIDI regions added, the control bar consistently displays the time signature as 4/4 and tempo as 120 BPM, and the top menu bar shows 'Logic Pro', 'Untitled - Tracks', and the system time progressing from 'Sun 14 Feb 19:59' to '20:00'.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with the Logic Pro X Interface displayed in its default dark theme, showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', both with their record enable buttons toggled on and off. The user interacts with the control bar, opening a dropdown menu to change the time display format from 'Beats & Project (Large)' to 'Beats & Time (Large)', which updates the timeline display accordingly. The user then clicks the play button, causing the playhead to move across the empty timeline, indicating playback. After stopping playback, the user navigates to the mixer view by clicking the 'Mix' button, revealing the channel strips for 'Inst 1', 'Inst 2', 'Stereo Out', and 'Master', each with faders, pan controls, and automation settings. The user briefly hovers over the 'Inst 1' channel strip before returning to the Tracks view. Throughout the segment, the interface remains static with no audio or MIDI regions added, and the control bar consistently displays the time signature as 4/4 and tempo as 120 BPM. The top menu bar shows the application name 'Logic Pro' and the project title 'Untitled - Tracks', with the system time indicating 'Sun 14 Feb 19:59' to '20:00'.

active_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with both tracks having their record enable buttons toggled on and off. The timeline remains empty, and the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format after user selection.

audio_updated_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface, actively being explained by a male voice-over who details its features, navigation, and shortcuts, including the 'X' key for the mixer view, and refers to the application by its name, 'Logic Pro'.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a user interacting with the dark-themed Logic Pro X Interface, where a speaker explains that the Mixer window can be made to disappear and then reopened by navigating to the 'Window' menu and selecting 'Open Mixer'. The speaker notes that this Mixer window, displaying vertical channel strips for 'Inst 1', 'Inst 2', 'Stereo Out', and 'Master' with faders and controls, opens separately and can be moved to a dual screen setup. After the user closes the Mixer and returns to the main Tracks view, the speaker introduces the Piano Roll, stating it opens by pressing 'P' (though visually the user clicks the 'Inst 1' track header to reveal it at the bottom). The empty Piano Roll grid, with piano keys and time divisions, is then explored by the user, hovering over editing options like 'Time Quantize' and 'Velocity'. The speaker further clarifies how to identify active windows by their blue highlighting and demonstrates keyboard shortcuts (Command + arrow keys) for zooming in and out within the interface, specifically mentioning their application for the Piano Roll editor. Throughout this, the timeline remains empty, and the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format, indicating no recording or playback.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with the user interacting with the Logic Pro X Interface, specifically navigating the 'Window' menu to select 'Open Mixer'. This action opens the Mixer window, which displays a vertical arrangement of channel strips for 'Inst 1', 'Inst 2', 'Stereo Out', and 'Master', each with faders, pan controls, and mute/solo buttons. The user then closes the Mixer window and returns to the main Tracks view. Next, the user clicks on the 'Inst 1' track header, which triggers the Piano Roll editor to appear at the bottom of the screen. The Piano Roll is initially empty, showing a grid with piano keys on the vertical axis and time on the horizontal axis. The user hovers over the Piano Roll, and the interface displays editing options such as 'Time Quantize', 'Strength', 'Swing', 'Scale Quantize', and 'Velocity' in the left-hand panel. The user does not input any notes but explores the interface, with the cursor moving over the Piano Roll grid and the track headers. The timeline remains empty throughout, and the control bar at the top continues to display time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format, indicating no recording or playback has occurred. The overall environment is a dark-themed DAW interface with clear, functional layout elements.

active_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with both tracks having their record enable buttons toggled on and off. The timeline remains empty, and the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format after user selection. The Piano Roll editor is opened for 'Inst 1', displaying an empty grid with piano keys and time divisions, along with editing parameters like Time Quantize, Strength, Swing, Scale Quantize, and Velocity in the left panel.

audio_updated_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface, featuring two software instrument tracks 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2' with record enable buttons. The speaker demonstrates how to open and close the Mixer window, open the Piano Roll editor (by pressing 'P' or clicking the track header), identify active windows via blue highlighting, and use Command + arrow key shortcuts for zooming in and out across the interface, including within the Piano Roll. The timeline remains empty, and the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format.
audio_updated_entities.Mixer window: This window, which displays vertical channel strips for 'Inst 1', 'Inst 2', 'Stereo Out', and 'Master' with faders, pan controls, and mute/solo buttons, is explained by the speaker as opening separately and being movable to a second screen for dual-monitor setups.
audio_updated_entities.Piano Roll editor: An empty grid with piano keys on the vertical axis and time on the horizontal axis, which the speaker explains can be opened by pressing 'P' (though visually opened by clicking the 'Inst 1' track header). The speaker also details that Command + arrow keys can be used for zooming in and out specifically within this editor, which displays editing options like 'Time Quantize', 'Strength', 'Swing', 'Scale Quantize', and 'Velocity' in its left-hand panel.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens on the dark-themed Logic Pro X Interface, displaying two software instrument tracks, 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with their record enable buttons being toggled on and off. The speaker states, "Abhi piano roll band kar dete hai," indicating the Piano Roll editor for 'Inst 1' is closed. The control bar at the top is set to 'Beats & Time (Large)' format. The speaker then introduces the Loop Browser, saying, "aur yeh hai hamara loop ke liye... idhar loop ka jo hai browser hai pura," and highlights the extensive collection of Logic Pro loops categorized by instruments and genres. The user opens the Loop Browser, navigating the 'Instrument' tab and scrolling through various loops like '12 String Dream 01' and '12 String Dream 02', highlighting them for preview. A loop plays, which the speaker explains, "ye jo loop hai aapka tempo ke hisaab se ye khel raha abhi because ye project ka tempo leke ye loop khelta hai," adapting to the project's tempo. The user continues scrolling past 'Windy City Piano' and 'Wonderland Strings' before closing the browser, with the speaker emphasizing the utility of loops and mentioning a future tutorial. Finally, the speaker introduces the "Note Pad" as a place for project notes, and the user opens it under the 'Project' tab, typing "Project Tempo: 120 BPM" to set the project's tempo, with the sound of typing audible. Throughout, the user interacts with the software via mouse clicks and scrolling.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with the Logic Pro X Interface displayed, showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', both with their record enable buttons toggled on and off. The timeline remains empty, and the control bar at the top displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format after the user selects this option from the dropdown menu. The user then opens the Loop Browser, navigating through the 'Instrument' tab and scrolling through various loops such as '12 String Dream 01' and '12 String Dream 02', highlighting them to preview. The user scrolls further down the list, passing loops like 'Windy City Piano' and 'Wonderland Strings', before closing the Loop Browser. The user then opens the Note Pad under the 'Project' tab and types 'Project Tempo: 120 BPM', indicating the project's tempo setting. Throughout the segment, the interface remains dark-themed, and the user interacts with the software using mouse clicks and scrolling actions, focusing on setting up the project environment and exploring available loops.

active_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with both tracks having their record enable buttons toggled on and off. The timeline remains empty, and the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format after user selection. The Piano Roll editor is opened for 'Inst 1', displaying an empty grid with piano keys and time divisions, along with editing parameters like Time Quantize, Strength, Swing, Scale Quantize, and Velocity in the left panel.

audio_updated_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with both tracks having their record enable buttons toggled on and off. The timeline remains empty, and the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format after user selection. The Piano Roll editor for 'Inst 1' is initially open but then closed by the user, as indicated by the speaker saying 'Abhi piano roll band kar dete hai'.
audio_updated_entities.Loop Browser: A panel on the right side of the Logic Pro X interface that the speaker introduces as 'hamara loop ke liye' and 'loop ka browser', displaying a list of available audio loops categorized by instrument, genre, and descriptors. The speaker notes it contains 'kitne saare loops jo Logic Pro ke taraf se hi hai' and highlights options to filter by scale, signature, and tempo. The user scrolls through loops like '12 String Dream 01' and 'Windy City Piano', highlighting them to preview, and a loop is heard playing, which the speaker explains 'aapka tempo ke hisaab se ye khel raha abhi because ye project ka tempo leke ye loop khelta hai'. The speaker also mentions making a separate tutorial on loops.
audio_updated_entities.Note Pad: A text input area within the Logic Pro X interface under the 'Project' tab, which the speaker introduces as 'notepad' and explains is for writing 'apne projects' or notes. The user types 'Project Tempo: 120 BPM' to set the project's tempo, with the sound of typing audible.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the dark-themed Logic Pro X Interface, displaying 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2' software instrument tracks with their record enable buttons being toggled. The timeline is initially empty, and the control bar is set to 'Beats & Time (Large)'. A male speaker, speaking in Hindi, confirms, "Hamara ho gaya 120 BPM" (Our 120 BPM is done) as the user navigates to the 'Project' tab and opens the Note Pad. The speaker explains, "ab jo bhi details hai aapka project ke bare mein aap idhar likh sakte ho" (whatever details you have about your project, you can write them here), praising the Note Pad as a "bahut hi behtareen option" (very good option) for remembering project specifics. The user then types 'Project Tempo: 120 BPM' and 'Key Signature: C' into the Note Pad. The speaker then states, "Key signature C major," and points out, "Ye dekhiye idhar C major hai" (See, here it is C major) as the user clicks the 'Signature' dropdown in the control bar, selecting 'E major', which updates the interface. The speaker continues to highlight other UI elements like markers and tempo, mentioning future tutorials. The user then selects '120' from the 'Tempo' dropdown, causing a blue '120' tempo region to appear on the timeline. The speaker further explains how to set the song's key, then points out "global preferences" and confirms the current settings: "C major ka key mein hai, 4/4 time signature hai" (it's in C major key, 4/4 time signature). The user subsequently changes the key signature to 'A major' via the dropdown. The segment concludes with the Piano Roll editor opening for 'Inst 1', revealing an empty grid with piano keys and various editing parameters.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with the Logic Pro X Interface displayed, showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', both with their record enable buttons toggled on and off. The timeline remains empty, and the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format after user selection. The user navigates to the 'Project' tab and opens the Note Pad, typing 'Project Tempo: 120 BPM' to set the project's tempo. They then type 'Key Signature: C' to specify the key. Next, the user clicks on the 'Signature' dropdown in the control bar, revealing a list of key signatures, and selects 'E major'. The interface updates to reflect the new key signature. The user then clicks on the 'Tempo' dropdown, revealing options like '120', '6/8', '12/8', and 'Custom...', and selects '120' to confirm the tempo. The timeline now shows a blue tempo region labeled '120' spanning the entire visible area. The user then clicks on the 'Signature' dropdown again, selects 'A major', and the interface updates to reflect this new key signature. The Piano Roll editor is opened for 'Inst 1', displaying an empty grid with piano keys and time divisions, along with editing parameters like Time Quantize, Strength, Swing, Scale Quantize, and Velocity in the left panel.

active_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with both tracks having their record enable buttons toggled on and off. The timeline remains empty, and the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format after user selection. The Piano Roll editor is opened for 'Inst 1', displaying an empty grid with piano keys and time divisions, along with editing parameters like Time Quantize, Strength, Swing, Scale Quantize, and Velocity in the left panel.
active_entities.Note Pad: A text input area within the Logic Pro X interface under the 'Project' tab, where the user types 'Project Tempo: 120 BPM' to set the project's tempo and 'Key Signature: C' to specify the key. The Note Pad has a 'Done' button to save the input.

audio_updated_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface, visually displaying two software instrument tracks ('Inst 1', 'Inst 2') with record enable toggles, an empty timeline, and a control bar showing 'Beats & Time (Large)'. The interface updates dynamically as the user selects key signatures (E major, A major) and tempo (120 BPM). The male speaker's voice confirms settings like 'C major' and '4/4 time signature' are visible on the interface, and the Piano Roll editor is opened for 'Inst 1', displaying an empty grid with piano keys and editing parameters.
audio_updated_entities.Note Pad: A text input area within the Logic Pro X interface, accessed via the 'Project' tab. Visually, it shows typed text like 'Project Tempo: 120 BPM' and 'Key Signature: C'. The male speaker, speaking in Hindi, describes it as a 'very good option' for writing down project details to aid memory, stating, 'whatever details you have about your project, you can write them here.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Logic Pro X Interface, displaying 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2' software instrument tracks with record enable buttons toggled. A male speaker with a Hindi accent explains that the key signature can be changed, demonstrating by selecting 'D minor' then 'C major', noting that any loops will then play according to the new key. The Loop Browser appears on the right, showing various categorized loops as the user scrolls through options like 'Irish Poly Harp 01' and 'Jack Impovy 01'. A 'DJ Neo subscribe to my channel' overlay with a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button briefly appears. The speaker then guides on loading instruments, prompting the user to open the Library panel, navigate to 'Piano', and select 'Yamaha Grand Piano'. A loading message appears, and 'Inst 1' updates to 'Yamaha Grand Piano'. The user switches to the Mixer view, revealing multiple channel strips with effects like 'Channel EQ' and 'Tape Delay'. The speaker highlights that Logic Pro has pre-configured settings, including bus sends for auxiliary channels with effects like delay, as the user adjusts the 'Bus 4' send on the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track, with the Master channel and 'Bnce' fader visible.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with the Logic Pro X Interface displaying two software instrument tracks, 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', both with record enable buttons toggled. The user interacts with the key signature dropdown, selecting 'D minor' before switching to 'C major'. The Loop Browser appears on the right, showing a list of loops categorized by instrument and genre, with the user scrolling through options like 'Irish Poly Harp 01' and 'Jack Impovy 01'. A 'DJ Neo subscribe to my channel' overlay with a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button appears briefly. The user then opens the Library panel, navigates to the 'Piano' category, and selects 'Yamaha Grand Piano', which loads into 'Inst 1' with a loading message. The track name updates to 'Yamaha Grand Piano', and the user switches to the Mixer view, revealing multiple channel strips with effects like 'Channel EQ' and 'Tape Delay'. The user adjusts the 'Bus 4' send on the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track, and the Mixer displays various output and automation controls, including 'Stereo Out' and 'Bnce' faders, with the Master channel visible at the far right.

active_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with both tracks having their record enable buttons toggled on and off. The timeline remains empty, and the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format after user selection. The Piano Roll editor is opened for 'Inst 1', displaying an empty grid with piano keys and time divisions, along with editing parameters like Time Quantize, Strength, Swing, Scale Quantize, and Velocity in the left panel.
active_entities.Loop Browser: A panel on the right side of the Logic Pro X interface that displays a list of available audio loops categorized by instrument, genre, and descriptors, with options to filter by scale, signature, and tempo. The user scrolls through loops like '12 String Dream 01' and 'Windy City Piano', highlighting them for preview.
active_entities.DJ NEO Logo Animation: A dark, particle-filled animation featuring a gold 'DJ NEO' logo with the tagline 'LEARN ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' and a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button at the bottom.
active_entities.Intro Title Screen: A black screen with bold red and white text reading 'LOGIC PRO INTRO CLASS #CLASS-1', serving as a title card for the tutorial.

audio_updated_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface where a male speaker with a Hindi accent guides the user through changing key signatures, loading instruments, and viewing pre-configured mixer settings. It displays two software instrument tracks, 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with record enable buttons toggled. The user interacts with the key signature dropdown, selecting 'D minor' then 'C major'. The Piano Roll editor is opened for 'Inst 1', displaying an empty grid with piano keys and time divisions, along with editing parameters like Time Quantize, Strength, Swing, Scale Quantize, and Velocity in the left panel.
audio_updated_entities.Loop Browser: A panel on the right side of the Logic Pro X interface that displays a list of available audio loops categorized by instrument, genre, and descriptors. The speaker explains that loops will play according to the selected key. The user scrolls through loops like '12 String Dream 01' and 'Windy City Piano', highlighting them for preview.
audio_updated_entities.DJ NEO Logo Animation: A dark, particle-filled animation featuring a gold 'DJ NEO' logo with the tagline 'LEARN ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' and a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button at the bottom, appearing briefly during the speaker's narration.
audio_updated_entities.Mixer View: The Mixer interface in Logic Pro X showing multiple channel strips with input, sends, output, and automation controls. The speaker highlights that Logic Pro has pre-configured settings, including bus sends for auxiliary channels with effects like delay. The 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track is visible with effects like 'Channel EQ' and 'Tape Delay', and the user adjusts the 'Bus 4' send. The Master channel is shown at the far right with a 'Bnce' fader, and the view includes 'Stereo Out' and 'Read' indicators for each track.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Logic Pro X interface, displaying 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Inst 2' software instrument tracks, both with record enable buttons toggled. A male voice speaking Hindi explains that reverb is also applied to the track, stating his preference for personal settings over presets, but demonstrating how to work with the interface. He then adjusts the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track's channel strip, including MIDI and Audio FX, and mentions the need to create an envelope to draw MIDI. Following his instruction to "right-click or two-finger click on Mac" to "create empty MIDI region," a green MIDI region labeled 'Yamaha Grand Piano' appears on the timeline. The speaker explains how to extend and loop the region, then extends it to 4 bars, stating the goal is to create a four-bar chord progression. He then double-clicks the region, causing the Piano Roll editor to open, revealing an empty grid. The speaker announces he will create a "small chord progression in C major." He then clicks to place three notes on the C3 key, identifying them as "C," "E," and "G" to form a "C major" chord, which are then selected and dragged to extend their duration. The editing panel on the left shows parameters like Time Quantize set to 1/16 Note and Velocity at 80, while the timeline displays the project in 4/4 time at 120 BPM.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with the Logic Pro X interface displaying two software instrument tracks, 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Inst 2', both with their record enable buttons toggled. The user interacts with the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track, adjusting its channel strip settings including MIDI FX and Audio FX, and then right-clicks to create a new MIDI region. A green MIDI region labeled 'Yamaha Grand Piano' appears on the timeline, which the user then extends to a length of 4 bars. The user double-clicks the region to open the Piano Roll editor, revealing an empty grid with piano keys and time divisions. In the Piano Roll, the user clicks to place three notes on the C3 key, which are then selected and dragged to extend their duration. The editing panel on the left shows parameters like Time Quantize set to 1/16 Note, Strength at 100, Swing at 0, Scale Quantize off, and Velocity at 80. The timeline displays the project in 4/4 time with a tempo of 120 BPM, and the control bar shows the time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format.

active_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Inst 2', with both tracks having their record enable buttons toggled on and off. The timeline initially remains empty, then a green MIDI region is created and extended to 4 bars. The control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format after user selection. The Piano Roll editor is opened for 'Yamaha Grand Piano', displaying an empty grid with piano keys and time divisions, along with editing parameters like Time Quantize, Strength, Swing, Scale Quantize, and Velocity in the left panel.

audio_updated_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface, where a male voice speaking Hindi guides the user through creating and editing MIDI regions, explaining actions like extending and looping, and opening the Piano Roll editor. The interface shows two software instrument tracks, 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Inst 2', with record enable buttons toggled, and displays project settings like 4/4 time and 120 BPM.
audio_updated_entities.Mixer View: The Mixer interface in Logic Pro X, where the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track's channel strip is visible with effects like 'Channel EQ' and 'Tape Delay'. The speaker's voice mentions that "reverb is also given" on the track, aligning with the visual adjustment of the 'Bus 4' send, and expresses a preference for custom settings over presets.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Logic Pro X Interface, a dark-themed digital audio workstation, where a male speaker with a Hindi accent begins by describing a "one-time chord progression" as the user edits MIDI notes on a 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track in the Piano Roll editor. Piano notes are heard playing as the user drags and adjusts note positions and velocities, then uses Time Quantize settings for precise alignment. The speaker continues, mentioning "then a GBD" and "then DFA," with corresponding piano notes playing. After editing, the speaker says, "Now let's play this," and a four-bar chord progression is heard playing on the piano. The speaker confirms, "This is our four-bar chord progression that we created here." The user then navigates to the track list and creates a new software instrument track named 'Inst 2'. The speaker explains, "And I want to choose my personal instrument now," leading to the 'New Track Dialog Box' appearing, where 'Studio Strings' is selected and assigned to 'Inst 2'. The 'Studio Strings Plugin Interface' then appears, showing controls for articulation, dynamics, and volume, with 'Violins 1' selected. The speaker elaborates on instrument selection, noting that choosing from certain areas provides "totally completely raw" sounds, while other selections allow Logic Pro to add its own effects. The user switches to the 'Library' panel, browsing sound categories like 'Bass', 'Drum Kit', and 'Piano', before returning to the track view. The timeline shows the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' region extended to 4 bars, and the control bar displays the time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format. The user interacts with the 'Mixer View', adjusting the 'Bus 4' send on the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track, with the Master channel visible, all within the dark-themed interface with green MIDI regions and clear labels.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with the Logic Pro X Interface displaying a single 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track in the Piano Roll editor, where the user edits MIDI notes by dragging and adjusting their positions and velocities. The user selects and modifies individual notes, changing their timing and duration, and uses the Time Quantize settings to align them precisely. After editing, the user navigates to the track list and creates a new software instrument track named 'Inst 2'. The user then opens the 'New Track Dialog Box' to add a new instrument, selects 'Studio Strings' from the 'Software Instrument' options, and assigns it to 'Inst 2'. The Studio Strings plugin interface appears, showing controls for articulation, dynamics, and volume, with the user selecting 'Violins 1' as the articulation. The user then switches to the 'Library' panel, browsing through sound categories like 'Bass', 'Drum Kit', and 'Piano', before returning to the track view to continue editing. The timeline shows the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' region extended to 4 bars, and the control bar displays the time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format. The user interacts with the Mixer View, adjusting the 'Bus 4' send on the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track, and the Master channel is visible with a 'Bnce' fader. The interface remains dark-themed throughout, with green MIDI regions and clear labels for tracks and parameters.

audio_updated_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface where a male speaker with a Hindi accent guides the user through editing MIDI notes for a 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track in the Piano Roll editor, playing piano notes as he describes the process. The user adjusts note positions, velocities, and applies Time Quantize settings. The speaker then explains the creation of a four-bar chord progression, which is heard playing. The user creates a new software instrument track named 'Inst 2', and the speaker discusses choosing instruments, differentiating between raw sounds and those with Logic Pro's built-in effects. The interface shows the 'New Track Dialog Box' and the 'Studio Strings Plugin Interface' as the user selects 'Violins 1' articulation. The user also browses the 'Library' panel and interacts with the 'Mixer View', adjusting a 'Bus 4' send on the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track, all while the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format and green MIDI regions are visible.
audio_updated_entities.New Track Dialog Box: A pop-up window in Logic Pro X, appearing after the speaker discusses choosing a new instrument, with tabs for 'Software Instrument', 'Audio', and 'Drummer', currently set to 'Software Instrument' with 'Empty Channel Strip' selected and the 'Create' button clicked to finalize track creation.
audio_updated_entities.Mixer View: The Mixer interface in Logic Pro X, shown as the user progresses through the instrument selection and editing workflow, displaying multiple channel strips. The 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track is visible with effects like 'Channel EQ' and 'Tape Delay', and the user adjusts the 'Bus 4' send. The Master channel is shown at the far right with a 'Bnce' fader, and the view includes 'Stereo Out' and 'Read' indicators for each track.
audio_updated_entities.Studio Strings Plugin Interface: A dark-themed plugin window for 'Studio Strings', appearing after the speaker's discussion about selecting an instrument, featuring a violin graphic and controls for Articulation (set to 'Violins 1'), Dynamics via CC, Last Played Articulation, Cutoff, Resonance, Volume, Attack, and Release. The interface includes navigation buttons, Copy, Paste, Undo, Redo, and a View percentage indicator.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens on the Logic Pro X Interface, displaying 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Inst 2' software instrument tracks with their record enable buttons toggled. A male speaker's voice is heard, initially saying "Studio Strings" and "mono," then stating in Hindi, "Look, here's my Studio Strings," as a green MIDI region is created on the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track and extended to four bars. The speaker continues in Hindi, explaining, "And I'll copy-paste this same thing there," and provides instructions: "To copy, press and hold Alt or the Option key, click and drag down," as the MIDI region is copied to the 'Inst 2' track. The Piano Roll editor is opened for the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track, showing an empty grid and editing parameters. The speaker then confirms, "And this instrument is now Strings," as 'Inst 2' is renamed 'Strings' via a context menu. The Studio Strings Plugin Interface appears, showing controls for Articulation set to 'Violins 1', Dynamics, Cutoff, Resonance, Volume, Attack, and Release, accompanied by a violin graphic. The speaker explains the origin of the MIDI, "And look here, we actually took the chords from Yamaha Grand Piano, so it will show here." He then guides the user on renaming, "If you want to change this, right-click or two-finger click, and you can rename it, Name and Color, Rename Regions, Name Regions by Tracks," as the user right-clicks the 'Strings' region to rename it. The track name in the left panel also updates to 'Strings'. The speaker confirms, "Look, this is now according to the Strings tracks." In the Mixer View, the user adjusts the volume fader for the 'Strings' track, reducing it significantly, while the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)'. A string melody, accompanied by piano, begins to play, demonstrating the audio output, with the speaker continuing to adjust the volume fader. The speaker concludes in Hindi, "So this is what I just showed you, how we create instruments in this and basically how we can use it."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment begins with the Logic Pro X Interface displaying two software instrument tracks: 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Inst 2', both with their record enable buttons toggled. The user creates a green MIDI region on the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track, extending it to four bars. The Piano Roll editor is opened for this track, showing an empty grid with piano keys and time divisions, alongside editing parameters like Time Quantize, Strength, Swing, Scale Quantize, and Velocity. The user then copies the MIDI region to the 'Inst 2' track, which is renamed 'Strings' via a context menu. The Studio Strings Plugin Interface appears, showing controls for Articulation (set to 'Violins 1'), Dynamics via CC, Cutoff, Resonance, Volume, Attack, and Release, with a violin graphic. The user adjusts the volume fader for the 'Strings' track in the Mixer View, reducing it significantly. The control bar at the top displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format, and the timeline cursor moves across the grid as the user navigates. The user right-clicks on the 'Strings' region, opens a context menu, and selects 'Rename Regions' to rename the region to 'Strings'. The track name in the left panel is also updated to 'Strings', and the user continues to adjust the volume fader, demonstrating the effect on the audio output.

audio_updated_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface showing 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Inst 2' software instrument tracks, both with record enable buttons toggled. A green MIDI region is created and extended to 4 bars. The Piano Roll editor is opened for 'Yamaha Grand Piano', displaying an empty grid with piano keys and time divisions, along with editing parameters. The control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)'. The interface is where the speaker demonstrates creating and managing instruments, with his voice guiding the actions in Hindi, and it produces the string melody and piano accompaniment heard during playback.
audio_updated_entities.Studio Strings Plugin Interface: A dark-themed plugin window for 'Studio Strings' with a violin graphic, featuring controls for Articulation (set to 'Violins 1'), Dynamics via CC, Cutoff, Resonance, Volume, Attack, and Release. The speaker explicitly names it, saying "Studio Strings" and "Look, here's my Studio Strings" in Hindi, confirming its appearance and function.
audio_updated_entities.Mixer View: The Mixer interface in Logic Pro X showing multiple channel strips. The 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track is visible, and the user adjusts the volume fader for the 'Strings' track, significantly reducing it, while the speaker's voice explains the process and the resulting change in the audio output.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a static view of the Logic Pro X Interface, a dark-themed digital audio workstation displaying 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Strings' software instrument tracks, both record-enabled. Two green MIDI regions, each four bars long, are visible on the timeline, while the control bar shows a 4/4 time signature, 120 BPM tempo, and C major key. The user's cursor hovers, but no active editing occurs. The left panel reveals the track mixer, with the 'Strings' track selected and its volume fader set to -11.2 dB. Throughout this visual, a male voice, speaking in Hindi, provides an overview of Logic Pro X, describing it as a powerful digital audio workstation where "many things can be made and done," and stating that "most of his production happens in Logic Pro these days." He also mentions using FL Studio for specific sound needs. As the speaker concludes by encouraging viewers to "subscribe, like, comment," and "follow" for more tutorials, a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button appears at the bottom right. This is followed by a transition to the 'DJ NEO Logo Animation', a white screen featuring a red YouTube play button, the bold red text 'DJ NEO', and the tagline 'ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' with a prompt to 'SUBSCRIBE FOR MORE VIDEOS LIKE THIS', marking the end of the tutorial and the beginning of the outro.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with a view of the Logic Pro X Interface, showing two software instrument tracks: 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Strings', both with their record enable buttons toggled on. The timeline displays two green MIDI regions, each spanning four bars, indicating that MIDI data has been recorded or placed. The control bar at the top shows the time signature as 4/4, tempo at 120 BPM, and key signature as C major. The user's cursor hovers over the interface, suggesting interaction, but no active editing occurs. The left panel shows the track mixer with volume faders and pan controls, and the 'Strings' track is selected, with its volume fader set to -11.2 dB. The interface remains static for the majority of the segment, with no visible changes to the MIDI regions or track settings. Towards the end, a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button appears at the bottom right, followed by a transition to a white screen with a red YouTube play button and the text 'DJ NEO' along with the tagline 'ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' and a prompt to 'SUBSCRIBE FOR MORE VIDEOS LIKE THIS'. This marks the end of the tutorial segment and the beginning of the outro animation.

active_entities.Logic Pro X Interface: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Strings', with both tracks having their record enable buttons toggled on. The timeline displays two green MIDI regions, each spanning four bars. The control bar shows time signature 4/4, tempo 120 BPM, and key signature C major. The left panel includes track mixer controls with volume faders and pan settings, and the 'Strings' track is selected with its volume fader at -11.2 dB. The interface remains static with no active editing during the segment.

Below it, the tagline 'ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' and the prompt 'SUBSCRIBE FOR MORE VIDEOS LIKE THIS' are displayed in smaller black text. This serves as the outro animation, replacing the previous Logic Pro X interface.

The timeline displays two green MIDI regions, each spanning four bars. The control bar shows time signature 4/4, tempo 120 BPM, and key signature C major. The left panel includes track mixer controls with volume faders and pan settings, and the 'Strings' track is selected with its volume fader at -11.2 dB. The interface remains static with no active editing during the segment, while the speaker discusses its capabilities and his usage.

entity_library.Intro Title Screen: A black screen with bold red and white text reading 'LOGIC PRO INTRO CLASS #CLASS-1', serving as a title card for the tutorial whose purpose is explained by DJ Neo in the accompanying audio.
entity_library.Loop Browser: A panel on the right side of the Logic Pro X interface that displays a list of available audio loops categorized by instrument, genre, and descriptors. The speaker explains that loops will play according to the selected key. The user scrolls through loops like '12 String Dream 01' and 'Windy City Piano', highlighting them for preview.
entity_library.Note Pad: A text input area within the Logic Pro X interface, accessed via the 'Project' tab. Visually, it shows typed text like 'Project Tempo: 120 BPM' and 'Key Signature: C'. The male speaker, speaking in Hindi, describes it as a 'very good option' for writing down project details to aid memory, stating, 'whatever details you have about your project, you can write them here.'
